frame_analyses.description: At 11 seconds, both instruments in field near drain. Left instrument (black, serrated jaws) and right instrument (silver) engaged with needle and suture. Curved needle visible with suture draped across drain. Fast entry.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Instrument entry comparable to recent attempts — fast and confident. Needle loading beginning promptly.

frame_analyses.description: At 33 seconds, both instruments working on the Penrose drain at close range. Needle driving in progress — needle being passed through drain tissue. Drain edges being manipulated.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Suture placement underway. Instruments appear coordinated but working slowly.

frame_analyses.description: At 55 seconds, instruments still working near drain surface. Suture thread visible. Needle being positioned or driven through second mark.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Still in suture placement at 55 seconds. Extended placement phase — comparable to the worst attempts (postV6: 62s, postV8: 62s).

frame_analyses.description: At 78 seconds, suture has been placed through drain. Both suture ends visible extending from drain. Left instrument (serrated jaws visible) and right instrument positioned. Suture loop visible above drain.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Suture placement completing or just completed at ~78 seconds. This is the LONGEST suture placement in the entire series — approximately 67 seconds. Significantly worse than V7/V9 (30-33s).

frame_analyses.description: At 100 seconds, throw wrapping visible. Right instrument elevated with suture looped around it. Left instrument near drain holding free end. Classic surgeon's knot wrapping configuration.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: First throw wrapping in progress. The double-wrap pattern is visible. Reaching first throw at ~90-95s is comparable to the fatigue-affected V8 timing (80s).

frame_analyses.description: At 122 seconds, left instrument elevated at upper left with suture loop. Right instrument working near drain. Wrapping for second throw in progress.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Second throw in progress. Left instrument leading the wrap suggests hand switch from throw 1 (right-led). Instrument positions reversed from frame 9.

frame_analyses.description: At 133 seconds, both instruments near drain with suture. Third throw wrapping or tightening in progress.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Third throw phase. Instruments working close to drain surface.

frame_analyses.description: At 155 seconds, left instrument elevated with suture loop. Right instrument near drain. Continued tightening of third throw.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Third throw extended — still working at 155 seconds. The throw phase from 130-165s (~35 seconds) is the longest third throw in the post-training series.

frame_analyses.description: At 178 seconds, both instruments holding suture taut between them above the drain. Left instrument near drain, right instrument elevated. Suture cutting beginning — positioning suture for cutting.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Early cutting phase. Instruments positioning suture tails for cutting.

frame_analyses.description: At 189 seconds, long suture tail visible extending upward. Right instrument (appears to be scissors) holding the tail. Left instrument near drain. Cutting first suture end.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Cutting progressing slowly. Long suture tail suggests first cut may not yet be complete.

frame_analyses.description: At 211 seconds, SCISSORS clearly visible on left side (ring handles, sharp blades). Right instrument near drain holding suture. STILL CUTTING at 211 seconds — extremely extended cutting phase.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Cutting phase has consumed over 45 seconds. This matches or exceeds post-4's problematic 44-second cutting phase. The trainee appears to be struggling with suture tail cutting.

frame_analyses.description: Final frame at 221 seconds. Instruments fully withdrawn. Completed knot visible on Penrose drain — dark suture forming an X pattern on the drain surface. The drain slit shows a VISIBLE GAP — the upper portion of the slit is not fully approximated. Short suture tails near knot. Drain remains on suture block (not avulsed).
frame_analyses.technique_notes: WORST TIME IN THE ENTIRE SERIES at ~215 seconds. The gap confirms inconsistent knot tension. The knot itself appears compact but the closure is only partial. This is 73 seconds slower than V7/V9 peak performance.

knot_assessments.notes: Frame 9 shows double-loop wrapping around right instrument consistent with surgeon's knot. Confidence moderate.
Frame 11 shows left instrument leading the wrap — reversed from throw 1 (right). Hand switch confirmed. Confidence moderate.
Third throw completed but the extended phase (35s) and final gap in drain slit suggest the throw was not optimally tightened. Confidence low.

technique_summary: This tenth video is the WORST performance in the entire training series — at 215 seconds with a visible drain gap, it exceeds even V8 (208s/379). Every phase except needle loading and the first two throws has regressed: suture placement at 67 seconds is the worst ever recorded (previous worst: 62s in V6/V8), the third throw at 35 seconds is extended, and the cutting phase at 50 seconds is the longest in the series (previous worst: 44s in post-4). The final result shows a partial gap confirming knot tension issues. Needle loading remains locked at 13 seconds and throw phases 1-2 are actually at good speed (22-23s), indicating the core throw mechanics are retained. The extreme time loss is concentrated in suture placement (+34s vs V7) and cutting (+22s vs V7). This almost certainly represents severe fatigue if performed in the same session, or a regression that demands attention to suture placement and cutting drills.
improvement_suggestions: CRITICAL: If this was the same session as V9 — STOP PRACTICING. The 72-second regression from V9 (143s → 215s) is the strongest fatigue signal yet. Session should have ended after V9.
Suture placement at 67 seconds is the worst ever — when fresh, the trainee achieves 30-33s. This is not a skill problem, it's likely a fatigue/focus problem.
Cutting at 50 seconds consumed 23% of total time — consider dedicated scissors drill sessions when rested
Third throw at 35 seconds also regressed — fatigue appears to compound in later phases
Recommendation: limit sessions to 3-4 attempts maximum. The V7→V8→V9 pattern showed V7 was fresh/peak, V8 was fatigued, V9 recovered. Adding V10 just repeated the V8 fatigue crash.
strengths: Needle loading maintained at 13 seconds — deeply ingrained skill unaffected by fatigue
First and second throw speeds (23s, 22s) remain at good levels — core knot tying mechanics retained
Hand switch confirmed between throws 1 and 2 (right → left)
All three throws completed in correct sequence
Drain not avulsed — tissue handling remains safe even under significant fatigue

key_observations: NEW WORST TIME: 215s (previous worst V8: 208s, pre-training: 198s)
Suture placement worst ever: 67s (V7 best: 30s, pre: 50s)
Cutting worst ever: 50s (V7 best: 30s, post-2 best: 18s)
73-second regression from V7 peak — most severe fatigue crash
Needle loading (13s) and early throws (22-23s) unaffected — fatigue hits complex phases
Gap returned — partial closure consistent with fatigue pattern
Strong recommendation: end session after 3 attempts max; benchmark remains V7/V9 at 142-143s
